questions.question: What is type narrowing in TypeScript?

choices.a: Removing properties from an object type to make it smaller
choices.b: The process where TypeScript refines a broad type to a more specific type based on runtime checks in code
choices.c: Casting a type using `as` to a narrower type
choices.d: Reducing the number of union members at compile time only

questions.question: What is a discriminated union and why is it preferred for modeling state?

choices.a: A union where all members share a common literal type property (the discriminant), enabling exhaustive narrowing
choices.b: A union of exactly two types
choices.c: A union that uses `instanceof` to distinguish members
choices.d: A union where each member must implement the same interface

questions.question: What is a type predicate and when should you write one?

choices.a: A function that returns `boolean` and has return type `x is T`, telling TypeScript the argument is T when it returns true
choices.b: A compiler option to enable strict type checking
choices.d: An assertion that a value is non-null

questions.question: How do you perform exhaustiveness checking in TypeScript?

choices.a: Use `Object.keys()` to verify all keys are handled
choices.b: In the default/else branch of a switch, assign the value to a `never` variable — TypeScript errors if not all cases are handled
choices.d: TypeScript automatically warns on incomplete switch statements

questions.question: What is the difference between `typeof` narrowing and `instanceof` narrowing?

choices.a: `typeof` works with any type; `instanceof` only works with numbers
choices.b: `typeof` checks primitive types (string, number, boolean, etc.); `instanceof` checks class/constructor identity in the prototype chain
choices.c: They are interchangeable — both do the same thing
choices.d: `instanceof` is a TypeScript-only feature; `typeof` is JavaScript

questions.question: What does the `in` operator do for type narrowing?

choices.a: It checks if a value exists inside an array
choices.b: It narrows a union type by checking if a specific property name exists on the value
choices.c: It is only for iterating object keys with `for...in`
choices.d: It is not recognized by TypeScript for narrowing

questions.question: What is an assertion function and how does it differ from a type predicate?

choices.a: They are the same thing with different syntax
choices.b: Assertion functions narrow the type for the REST of the scope (not just inside an if block); they throw if the condition fails
choices.c: Assertion functions only work with `class` instances
choices.d: Assertion functions return a boolean; type predicates return void

questions.question: What is control flow analysis in TypeScript?

choices.a: A runtime feature that tracks variable assignments at runtime
choices.b: TypeScript's ability to track type changes through conditional branches, assignments, and early returns throughout a function's code
choices.c: A linting rule that prevents unreachable code
choices.d: The process of computing dependency graphs between modules

questions.explanation:
CONTROL FLOW ANALYSIS (CFA) is how TypeScript tracks what type a variable has at each point in the code:

```typescript
function example(x: string | null) {
  if (x === null) return; // early return
  // TypeScript knows x is string from here — null was eliminated
  x.toUpperCase(); // safe

  x = null; // re-assignment
  // Now x: null again
  x?.toString(); // TypeScript knows x might be null here
}
```

CFA tracks:
• if/else branches
• switch cases
• Early return/throw
• Assignment
• Logical operators (`&&`, `||`, `??`)
• Optional chaining

This is how TypeScript can give you precise types at every line without extra annotations.
